content:
1	---
2	name: blueprint-writer
3	description: Update one blueprint chapter to reflect strategy changes, fill missing definitions or theorems, or align prose with the current Lean structure. Plan-agent-dispatched; one writer per chapter. May spawn reference-retriever mid-session when drafting reveals a missing source.
4	write_domain: "blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex"
5	read_only: false
6	can_spawn: true
7	default_enabled: false
8	dispatcher_notes: |
9	  - Dispatch one writer per chapter that the most recent blueprint
10	    review (the blueprint-review subagent in your catalog, when
11	    present) flagged as incomplete, lacking proof detail, or missing
12	    multi-route coverage.
13	  - Each directive must be precise: strategy context (the slice that
14	    matters for this chapter), required definitions/theorems (with
15	    enough mathematical detail to formalize), references, and
16	    explicit out-of-scope items. Writers do NOT speculate beyond
17	    what the directive lists.
18	  - **NEVER instruct the writer to add ``\leanok`` or ``\mathlibok``
19	    markers** (no "after writing the block, add `\leanok`"; no
20	    "verification: confirm `\leanok` is present"). The writer's
21	    descriptor forbids it — ``\leanok`` is managed by the
22	    deterministic ``sync_leanok`` phase, ``\mathlibok`` by the review
23	    agent. A directive that asks the writer to add markers puts it in
24	    a rule conflict; the writer must obey its descriptor, so the
25	    "verification" instruction will appear to fail until sync_leanok
26	    runs. Leave marker concerns out of the writer's directive entirely.
27	  - **Authorize the retriever in the writer's --write-domain.** If
28	    the chapter might need fresh source material, dispatch the
29	    writer with TWO globs:
30	      --write-domain 'blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter>.tex'
31	      --write-domain 'references/**'
32	    The writer itself only edits its assigned chapter (its prompt
33	    body enforces that), but the second glob authorizes a child
34	    reference-retriever if the writer discovers it needs one. Omit
35	    `references/**` only when you are confident the writer will not
36	    need new sources (e.g. purely cleanup edits).
37	  - If the strategy has multiple viable routes and one route's chapters
38	    are missing, dispatch a writer per route to bring all routes to
39	    parity before any prover work begins. Do not push provers onto a
40	    route whose blueprint coverage is incomplete.
41	  - After a significant writer round, re-run the blueprint review
42	    in the same iteration (the relevant entry in your catalog) to
43	    confirm the updated blueprint is now sufficient — do not assume
44	    the writer fixed everything.
45	  - If a writer's report contains entries under "Strategy-modifying
46	    findings", STOP and update STRATEGY.md before any further Lean
47	    work this iter. The writer is telling you the prose surfaced a
48	    strategy-level issue.
49	  - If a writer's report includes child reference-retriever dispatches,
50	    skim the new `references/<slug>.md` files before using the
51	    writer's output — the writer relied on them, so should you.
52	---
53	
54	# Blueprint Writer
55	
56	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.
57	
58	## Your Job
59	
60	The plan agent has decided that a specific blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) needs to change — to reflect a strategic decision, fill a missing definition or theorem the project needs, or align the informal prose with the current Lean structure. Your directive tells you which chapter, what must be there, and what counts as out-of-scope.
61	
62	You only edit the **one chapter named in your directive**. Your declared `--write-domain` should reflect that (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`). You do NOT edit other chapters, `.lean` files, or `content.tex`. Cross-chapter inconsistencies you spot go in the report's "Notes for Plan Agent" section — you flag them, you don't fix them.
63	
64	## Directive Format
65	
66	```markdown
67	# Blueprint Writer Directive
68	
69	## Slug
70	<slug>
71	
72	## Target chapter
73	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
74	
75	## Strategy context
76	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted for you — typically a paragraph or two describing what this chapter must support in the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself; the plan agent gives you only what is relevant.>
77	
78	## Required content
79	- Definition <name>: <informal description of what it must define, with the mathematical content the prover needs in order to formalize>
80	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
81	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>
82	- ...
83	
84	## Out of scope
85	<things that look related but the plan agent does NOT want you to touch this round>
86	
87	## References
88	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>
89	- ...
90	
91	## Expected outcome
92	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
93	```
94	
95	If a section is omitted from your directive, the plan agent decided you don't need it. Don't speculate beyond what was given.
96	
97	## Chapter format
98	
99	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
100	
101	```latex
102	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
103	  \label{thm:some_label}
104	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
105	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
106	  Informal statement of the theorem, using standard mathematical notation.
107	\end{theorem}
108	
109	\begin{proof}
110	  \uses{thm:another_result}
111	  Step-by-step informal proof. Reference blueprint labels with \uses{...}
112	  so the dependency graph stays accurate.
113	\end{proof}
114	```
115	
116	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
117	
118	## Rules
119	
120	### What you CAN do
121	- Add new declaration blocks (definitions, lemmas, theorems, propositions, corollaries) under direction.
122	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.
123	- Add `\uses{...}` cross-references.
124	- Adjust `\lean{...}` hints when the directive names a new Lean target.
125	- Read `references/summary.md` and any reference that is in `references/` to ground your writing in the project's sources.
126	
127	### What you MUST do
128	- **Keep the chapter valid LaTeX.** Don't leave dangling `\begin{...}` without matching `\end{...}`. Compile-checking is the plan agent's responsibility but you must not introduce syntax errors.
129	- **Stay within your chapter.** Your declared write-domain is one `*.tex` file. The Archon CLI rejects writes outside it.
130	- **Define non-standard macros in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex`** before using them — but: that file is outside your write-domain, so you DON'T touch it. If the directive requires a new macro, you note in your report "needs macro `\foo`" and leave the LaTeX using the new command name; the plan agent adds the macro before next iter's typeset.
131	- **Use mathematical, not Lean-syntactic prose.** Describe the proof in the language of mathematics — definitions, set inclusions, ring maps, universal properties — not in Lean tactic syntax. The prover formalizes your math.
132	- **Document every change** in your report.
133	
134	### What you MUST NOT do
135	- **Do NOT add `\leanok` or `\mathlibok` markers.** Those are managed by the `sync_leanok` phase + the review agent — never by you.
136	- **Do NOT edit other chapters.** Even when you spot a related issue, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" instead of fixing it.
137	- **Do NOT edit `content.tex`** (the top-level blueprint file that `\input`s the chapters).
138	- **Do NOT edit `.lean` files** or any other state file.
139	- **Do NOT write Lean syntax** — keep the chapter mathematical, not syntactic.
140	- **Do NOT expand scope.** Stick to what the directive listed under "Required content".
141	
142	## Reading references
143	
144	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
145	
146	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
147	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
148	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
149	
150	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
151	
152	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
153	
154	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
155	
156	Conditions for dispatch:
157	
158	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
159	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
160	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
161	
162	Dispatch (Bash, foreground, in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
163	
164	```
165	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
166	  --name reference-retriever \
167	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
168	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
169	  --write-domain 'references/**'
170	```
171	
172	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
173	
174	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
175	
176	## Workflow
177	
178	1. Read your directive completely.
179	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references.
180	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
181	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow.
182	5. **If a needed source is missing from `references/`, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and wait for it to return before drafting the affected sections.
183	6. Make the edits.
184	7. Verify the file is still valid LaTeX at a glance (no unmatched begin/end, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`).
185	8. Write your report.
186	
187	## Logging
188	
189	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/blueprint-writer-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path under `task_results/<parent-slug>/` when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
190	
191	```markdown
192	# Blueprint Writer Report
193	
194	## Slug
195	<slug>
196	
197	## Status
198	<COMPLETE | INCOMPLETE>
199	<If INCOMPLETE: which required items could not be written and why.>
200	
201	## Target chapter
202	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
203	
204	## Changes Made
205	- **Added definition** `\definition`/`\label{def:foo}`/`\lean{Foo.bar}` — <one line on what it captures>
206	- **Added theorem** `\theorem`/`\label{thm:foo}` — <one line on statement>
207	  - Proof sketch added: <Y/N + brief shape>
208	- **Revised** `<existing label>` — <one line on what changed>
209	- ...
210	
211	## Cross-references introduced
212	- `\uses{thm:bar}` added in proof of `\thm:foo` — verify `thm:bar` exists in <chapter or this same one>
213	- ...
214	
215	## Macros needed (if any)
216	- `\foo{...}` — used in <where>; needs definition in `macros/common.tex`. NOT added by me (out of write-domain).
217	
218	## Reference-retriever dispatches (if any)
219	- slug `<child-slug>`: requested `<source>`. Status: COMPLETE / NOT_FOUND / PARTIAL. Summary at `references/<slug>.md`.
220	- ...
221	
222	## Notes for Plan Agent
223	- <any inconsistency I noticed in sibling chapters that the directive didn't cover>
224	- <any structural concern: e.g. "the chapter is now 800 lines, consider splitting">
225	- <any reference I had to guess at because the directive's reference section was incomplete>
226	
227	## Strategy-modifying findings
228	<populate this section ONLY if writing the chapter surfaced a need to
229	change the project's strategy itself — not just the chapter content.
230	Examples:
231	- A definition you were asked to write turns out to require a property
232	  that the strategy assumes is automatic but is not.
233	- A theorem you sketched is provable as stated, but its consequence
234	  used elsewhere in STRATEGY.md does not follow from it.
235	- A reference cited as authoritative actually requires a different
236	  setup than the strategy assumes.
237	
238	When this section is non-empty, the plan agent must update STRATEGY.md
239	before any further Lean work this iteration. Do NOT silently adapt the
240	chapter to paper over a strategy-level issue — surface it here.>
241	```
242	
243	## Return Value
244	
245	Your final assistant message:
246	
247	- One line: `<slug>: COMPLETE | INCOMPLETE — <one-sentence outcome>`
248	- The path to your full report.
249	
250	Keep the inline return short. The plan agent reads the full report.
251

1955	  Estimated build: $\sim 150$--$300$ LOC. Steps 1--2 ($\sim 30$--$50$ LOC) consume the standard-smooth presentation of $W$ via \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmooth.free\_kaehlerDifferential}. Step 3 ($\sim 60$--$120$ LOC) is the dominant cost: instantiating the affine-cover Mayer--Vietoris square (\cref{thm:Scheme_AffineCoverMVSquare_HModule_prime_sequence_exact}) on $\Omega_{C/k}^{\oplus g}$ and chasing the kernel. Steps 4--5 ($\sim 60$--$130$ LOC) consume the two project-internal helpers below.
1956	
1957	  \emph{Mathlib status.} The chart-side standard-smooth presentation: \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmooth.free\_kaehlerDifferential} and \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.rank\_kaehlerDifferential} are [verified] in \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Smooth.StandardSmoothCotangent}. The K\"ahler-derivation functoriality \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.map} / \texttt{relativeDifferentials'\_map\_d} is [verified] in \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Kaehler.Basic} / \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Differentials.Presheaf}. The chart-\v Cech kernel-equals-global-sections identification of Step 3 is supplied by the chart-algebra ($\alpha$) helper \cref{lem:chart_algebra_isPushout_of_affine_product} plus the abstract affine-cover Mayer--Vietoris sequence \cref{thm:Scheme_AffineCoverMVSquare_HModule_prime_sequence_exact} (\cref{chap:Cohomology_MayerVietoris}) instantiated on the 2-chart cover of Step 3.aux; the 2-chart cover construction itself consumes the \texttt{AffineCover} + \texttt{IsAffineOpen} + \texttt{LocalizationAway} API of \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme} + \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Localization.Away.Basic} (cf.\ Stacks Tag~0F8L for the construction). The two project-internal helpers \cref{lem:KaehlerDifferential_mem_range_algebraMap_of_D_eq_zero} and \cref{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field} discharge Steps 4 and 5 respectively.
1958	\end{proof}
1959	
1960	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1961	[(ii-$\beta$-aux) Constants of a smooth proper geometrically irreducible scheme are the base field]
1962	  \label{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field}
1963	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.constants_integral_over_base_field}
1964	  % NOTE (iter-146 review): the iter-146 Lean signature carries an
1965	  % explicit `[IsReduced X]` typeclass hypothesis (in addition to the
1966	  % `Smooth`, `IsProper`, `GeometricallyIrreducible` hypotheses listed
1967	  % in the prose). Mathlib snapshot `b80f227` lacks a general
1968	  % `Smooth (X → Spec k) ⇒ IsReduced X` lemma over a field (the closest
1969	  % is `Scheme.Hom.dense_smoothLocus_of_perfectField`, perfect-base
1970	  % only), so the project explicitly carries `[IsReduced X]` at every
1971	  % call site. Once the Mathlib gap closes, the explicit-`IsReduced`
1972	  % hypothesis will be derivable from `[Smooth (X → Spec k)]` and may
1973	  % be dropped. Same discipline already documented for `Rigidity.lean`.
1974	  % NOTE (iter-146 review): the iter-146 Lean closure formalises substeps
1975	  % (1)–(2) of the three-substep recipe below (`IrreducibleSpace` +
1976	  % `isIntegral_of_irreducibleSpace_of_isReduced` for (1);
1977	  % `isField_of_universallyClosed` + `finite_appTop_of_universallyClosed`
1978	  % for (2)). Substep (3) — the geometric-irreducibility base-change
1979	  % step that pins `dim_k Γ(X, ⊤) = 1` — remains a structured `sorry`
1980	  % at iter-146 close, scheduled for iter-147+ closure via the
1981	  % `IsBaseChange`-namespace + Stacks Tag 02KH idiom plus
1982	  % `Module.finrank_baseChange`.
1983	  % NOTE (iter-147 review): the iter-147 prover lane reduces the
1984	  % `RingHom.range (appTop.hom) = ⊤` goal to surjectivity of the
1985	  % appTop ring hom via `RingHom.range_eq_top`, and documents the
1986	  % full 7-step closure chain (a)–(g) as in-source comments inside
1987	  % the body. The chain pulls back `X` along the algebraic closure
1988	  % `\bar k`, applies `isField_of_universallyClosed` +
1989	  % `finite_appTop_of_universallyClosed` on `X_{\bar k}`, then
1990	  % `IsAlgClosed.algebraMap_bijective_of_isIntegral` to get
1991	  % `Γ(X_{\bar k}, ⊤) ≅ \bar k`, then flat base change of `Γ` to
1992	  % identify `Γ(X, ⊤) ⊗_k \bar k ≃ \bar k`, then
1993	  % `Module.finrank_baseChange` + `Subalgebra.bot_eq_top_iff_finrank_eq_one`
1994	  % to conclude. The substantive Mathlib gap — flat base change of
1995	  % `Γ` for a proper scheme over a field (step (e)) — is concentrated
1996	  % at the single residual `sorry`; the iter-148+ continuation lands a
1997	  % thin in-tree wrapper around `AlgebraicGeometry.IsBaseChange` + the
1998	  % `H^0` cohomology-base-change instance for the proper case.
1999	  Let $X$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible scheme over a field $k$, with structure morphism $\pi_X \colon X \to \Spec k$. Then the global sections form a finite-dimensional $k$-algebra that is integral over $k$ (via the canonical $\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X)$ from $\pi_X$); and the image of $\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X)$ coincides with $\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X)$:
2000	  \[
2001	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X) \;=\; \operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X)).
2002	  \]
2003	  Equivalently, $\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X) \cong k$ as $k$-algebras.
2004	\end{lemma}
2005	
2006	\begin{proof}
2007	
2008	\leanok
2009	  Three sub-facts chain to the conclusion:
2010	  \begin{enumerate}
2011	    \item \emph{$\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X)$ is a finite-dimensional $k$-vector space.} For $X$ proper over $\Spec k$, the structure-sheaf pushforward $(\pi_X)_{*}\,\mathcal O_X$ is coherent on $\Spec k$ (a finitely-generated $k$-module), and its global sections are $\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X)$. This is the standard properness-implies-finiteness-of-cohomology fact (Stacks Tag~0B5R / Hartshorne~II.5.19), available in Mathlib via the \texttt{IsProper}-namespace coherent-pushforward lemmas combined with \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.StructureSheaf} on global sections. As a corollary, $\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X)$ is a finite $k$-algebra.
2012	    \item \emph{$\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X)$ is an integral domain.} $X$ is smooth (hence reduced) and geometrically irreducible over $k$, so $X$ is in particular irreducible and reduced, hence integral. The structure sheaf of an integral scheme has integral-domain global sections (Mathlib: \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IsIntegral}-namespace lemmas on $\Gamma$).
2013	    \item \emph{A finite integral-domain $k$-algebra over a field is a finite field extension; geometric irreducibility forces dimension $1$.} Combining (1) and (2), $\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X)$ is a finite integral domain over the field $k$, hence is itself a field (a finite integral extension of a field is a field, Mathlib's \texttt{Algebra.IsIntegral.isField\_iff\_isField} in \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.IntegralClosure}), and a finite field extension of $k$. Geometric irreducibility of $X$ over $k$ propagates to $X_{\bar k} := X \times_k \Spec \bar k$ being integral and smooth proper over $\bar k$; therefore $\Gamma(X_{\bar k}, \mathcal O_{X_{\bar k}})$ is itself a finite field extension of $\bar k$, hence equals $\bar k$ (the algebraic closure has no proper finite extensions). The base-change identity $\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X) \otimes_k \bar k \cong \Gamma(X_{\bar k}, \mathcal O_{X_{\bar k}}) = \bar k$ (from flat base change of structure-sheaf global sections, Mathlib's \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IsBaseChange}-namespace lemmas combined with $\Gamma$-functor) forces $\dim_k \Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X) = 1$, hence $\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X) = k \cdot 1 = \operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X))$.
2014	  \end{enumerate}
2015	  The three sub-facts deliver $\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X) \cong k$, i.e.\ the displayed equality. Estimated build: $\sim 50$--$100$ LOC.
2016	
2017	  \emph{Mathlib status.} Properness-implies-finite-cohomology is [verified] in \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry} via the \texttt{IsProper}-namespace coherent-pushforward lemmas. The integral-domain conclusion from \emph{integral} schemes uses Mathlib's \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IsIntegral}-namespace lemmas on $\Gamma$ ([verified]). \texttt{Algebra.IsIntegral.isField\_iff\_isField} is [verified] in \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.IntegralClosure}. Flat base change of $\Gamma$ for a proper scheme is the standard Stacks Tag~02KH and is [verified in idiom] in Mathlib via the \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IsBaseChange}-namespace; the project may need a [gap]-fill thin wrapper to state the chart-$\bar k$ identification cleanly. The geometric-irreducibility-to-integral step is direct from \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GeometricallyIrreducible} combined with the smoothness-implies-reducedness instance.
2018	\end{proof}
2019	
2020	\begin{lemma}
2021	[(ii-$\beta$-aux-chart) Image of the base field is integrally closed in a chart of a smooth proper geometrically irreducible scheme]
2022	  \label{lem:KaehlerDifferential_constants_in_chart_of_proper_curve}
2023	  \uses{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field}
2024	  Let $X$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible scheme over a field $k$, let $V \subseteq X.\mathrm{left}$ be a non-empty affine open subset, and let $B := \Gamma(X, V)$ denote the chart-side ring (a $k$-algebra via the structure map $X \to \Spec k$, restricted to $V$). Then the image of $k$ inside $B$ via the canonical algebra map is integrally closed in $B$:
2025	  \[
2026	    \operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,B) \;\;\text{is integrally closed in } B.
2027	  \]
2028	  Equivalently, any $b \in B$ satisfying a monic polynomial relation over $\operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,B)$ already lies in $\operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,B)$.
2029	
2030	  \paragraph{Iter-146 scope note.} This lemma extracts the chart-of-smooth-proper-geometrically-irreducible-scheme hypothesis out of the proof of \cref{lem:KaehlerDifferential_mem_range_algebraMap_of_D_eq_zero} (where step (p3) of that proof previously appealed to it implicitly), concentrating the curve-specific (more precisely, smooth-proper-geom-irr-specific) input at a named lemma the iter-146 KDM ring-side prover lane can dispatch as a black box. The KDM lemma's signature is left at its current finite-type-$k$-algebra generality; consumers (notably \cref{lem:chart_algebra_df_zero_factors_through_constant_on_chart}) supply the chart-of-proper-curve hypothesis at their site by invoking this lemma where the descent step requires it.
2031	\end{lemma}
2032	
2033	\begin{proof}
2034	  \uses{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field}
2035	  By \cref{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field} the global-section ring $\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X)$ coincides with the image of the canonical $\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X)$; in particular $\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X)$ is a copy of $k$ inside $X$'s global structure sheaf.
2036	
2037	  The chart restriction $\rho_V \colon \Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X) \to B = \Gamma(X, V)$ is the canonical sheaf-restriction map. On a reduced source (smoothness of $X$ over $k$ implies reducedness, Mathlib's \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.SmoothScheme}-namespace lemmas combined with the standard reducedness-from-regular-local-rings instance), the structure-sheaf restriction along an open immersion to a non-empty affine open is injective (sheaf separatedness on a reduced source: \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IsOpenImmersion} family + \texttt{Scheme.Spec\_germ\_injective}-shape lemmas). Hence $\rho_V$ embeds the field $\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X) = k$ inside $B$ as $\operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,B)$.
2038

The proof is the 7-step closure chain (a)--(g) below, refining the iter-145 three-substep recipe by naming each Mathlib hook explicitly and concentrating the genuine substantive Mathlib gap at a single named step (e). The chain pulls $X$ back along the algebraic closure $\bar k$, applies the universally-closed field/finiteness chain on $X_{\bar k}$ to identify $\Gamma(X_{\bar k}, \mathcal O_{X_{\bar k}}) \cong \bar k$, then transports back to $k$ via flat base change of global sections.
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item[(a)] \emph{Algebraize and reduce to surjectivity.} Algebraize the structure-morphism appTop ring hom $\mathtt{appTop.hom} \colon \mathcal O_{\Spec k}(\top) \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X)$ and the canonical $\mathtt{\Gamma SpecIso}\,k \colon \mathcal O_{\Spec k}(\top) \cong k$ so that $\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X)$ is exhibited as a $k$-algebra. Convert the displayed goal $\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X) = \operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X))$ (equivalently $\mathtt{RingHom.range}\,(\mathtt{appTop.hom}) = \top$) to surjectivity of $\mathtt{appTop.hom}$ via Mathlib's \texttt{RingHom.range\_eq\_top}; the proof now targets \texttt{Function.Surjective $\Updownarrow$(appTop.hom)}.
    \item[(b)] \emph{Base change to $\bar k$ and stability of hypotheses.} Pull back $X$ along $\Spec.\mathtt{map}\,(\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,\bar k)$ to obtain $X_{\bar k} := X \times_{\Spec k} \Spec \bar k$. The four standing hypotheses propagate:
      \begin{itemize}
        \item \emph{Properness.} $\mathtt{IsProper}\,(X_{\bar k} \to \Spec \bar k)$ is stable under base change (Mathlib's \texttt{IsProper}-namespace base-change instance).
        \item \emph{Smoothness.} $\mathtt{Smooth}\,(X_{\bar k} \to \Spec \bar k)$ is stable under base change (Mathlib's \texttt{Smooth}-namespace base-change instance).
        \item \emph{Reducedness.} $\mathtt{IsReduced}\,X_{\bar k}$ follows from smoothness over an algebraically closed (hence in particular perfect) base: either via \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Smooth.isReduced\_of\_smooth} directly, or via the \texttt{Algebra.IsGeometricallyReduced} idiom applied to the new base.
        \item \emph{Irreducibility.} $\mathtt{IrreducibleSpace}\,X_{\bar k}$ follows from the project-standing \texttt{GeometricallyIrreducible} hypothesis applied to the new structure morphism $X_{\bar k} \to \Spec \bar k$ over the singleton base $\Spec \bar k$, via Mathlib's \texttt{GeometricallyIrreducible.irreducibleSpace\_of\_subsingleton}.
      \end{itemize}
      Hence $X_{\bar k}$ is integral, smooth, and proper over the algebraically closed field $\bar k$.
    \item[(c)] \emph{Properness gives a finite field over $\bar k$.} Apply \texttt{isField\_of\_universallyClosed} + \texttt{finite\_appTop\_of\_universallyClosed} to the structure morphism $X_{\bar k} \to \Spec \bar k$ (properness gives universally closed, which combined with the (b)-integral structure delivers both lemmas' hypotheses). Conclusion: $\Gamma(X_{\bar k}, \mathcal O_{X_{\bar k}})$ is a field, and the appTop ring hom $\bar k \to \Gamma(X_{\bar k}, \mathcal O_{X_{\bar k}})$ is finite (equivalently, integral).
    \item[(d)] \emph{Algebraic closure absorbs the finite field extension.} Apply \texttt{IsAlgClosed.algebraMap\_bijective\_of\_isIntegral} with $k := \bar k$ and $K := \Gamma(X_{\bar k}, \mathcal O_{X_{\bar k}})$: an integral extension of an algebraically closed field is bijective on $\mathtt{algebraMap}$. Therefore $\mathtt{algebraMap}\,\bar k\,\Gamma(X_{\bar k}, \mathcal O_{X_{\bar k}})$ is bijective, i.e.\ $\Gamma(X_{\bar k}, \mathcal O_{X_{\bar k}}) \cong \bar k$ canonically.
    \item[(e)] \emph{Flat base change of global sections (the substantive Mathlib gap).} Use flat base change of global sections for proper morphisms to identify
      \[
        \Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X) \otimes_k \bar k \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\; \Gamma(X_{\bar k}, \mathcal O_{X_{\bar k}}) \;=\; \bar k,
      \]
      where the iso is the natural base-change comparison and the right-hand equality is from (d). \emph{This is the single substantive Mathlib gap of the chain.} The relevant authoritative formulation is \emph{cohomology along proper morphisms commutes with flat base change}: Stacks Tag~02KH, specialised from $H^{i}$ to $H^{0} = \Gamma$ (equivalently EGA~IV.6 / Hartshorne~III.11). Iter-148 disambiguation: the gap is genuinely flat-base-change-of-$\Gamma$-for-proper-schemes ($\sim 250$--$500$ LOC if built from scratch through the full cohomology-base-change chain), \emph{not} a thin wrapper over \texttt{finite\_appTop\_of\_universallyClosed} alone. The lemma name ``constants\_integral'' is therefore misleading: the current Lean signature commits to surjectivity ($\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X) \cong k$), which is strictly stronger than the bare integrality of $\Gamma$ over $k$ that (c)+(d) would deliver in isolation and which requires the deeper cohomology-base-change machinery.
    \item[(f)] \emph{Dimension count.} Conclude $\dim_k \Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X) = 1$ by applying \texttt{Module.finrank\_baseChange} to the equality $\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X) \otimes_k \bar k = \bar k$ of (e): both sides have $\bar k$-dimension $1$, hence $\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X)$ has $k$-dimension $1$.
    \item[(g)] \emph{Surjectivity from dimension $1$.} Conclude that $\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X)$ is surjective via \texttt{Subalgebra.bot\_eq\_top\_iff\_finrank\_eq\_one} (or equivalently the $\mathtt{Module.finrank}$-on-$\top$-vs-$\bot$ argument over a trivial finite field extension). This delivers the displayed equality and discharges the surjectivity reduction set up in (a).
  \end{enumerate}
  The earlier iter-145 three-substep recipe is recovered by collapsing (a)--(d) into substeps (1)+(2) (the geometrically-irreducible-stable integral structure plus the universally-closed field/finiteness conclusion on $X_{\bar k}$) and (e)--(g) into substep (3) (the geometric-irreducibility base-change conclusion $\dim_k \Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X) = 1$). The 7-step refinement names each Mathlib hook so the iter-148+ prover lane can target step (e) --- the genuine substantive gap --- without ambiguity about the surrounding scaffolding. Estimated build: $\sim 50$--$100$ LOC combined for steps (a)--(d) and (f)--(g); step (e) is the dominant cost at $\sim 250$--$500$ LOC if Mathlib's cohomology-base-change machinery must be developed from scratch through Stacks Tag~02KH, or substantially less if Mathlib has by iter-148+ shipped a proper-$H^{0}$ base-change instance as a thin specialisation.

The substantive Mathlib gap is concentrated at step (e), flat base change of $\Gamma$ for proper schemes (Stacks Tag~02KH, specialised to $H^{0}$); Mathlib snapshot \texttt{b80f227} ships the abstract \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IsBaseChange}-namespace idiom but does not directly expose the proper-$H^{0}$ instance, so the in-tree build either (i) lands the Stacks 02KH formalisation in full ($\sim 250$--$500$ LOC) or (ii) supplies a thin in-tree wrapper if Mathlib's cohomology-base-change machinery has matured by iter-148+.
